claims.claim_text: GitHub's October 2018 incident caused roughly 24 hours of degraded service after a cross-datacenter failover left database state inconsistent.
claims.span_hint: line 27 | section: Why This Module Matters
claims.rationale: The GitHub post-incident analysis directly supports the timeline and database-topology failure, but the host is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The outage caused millions in lost productivity and SLA penalties.
claims.span_hint: line 27 | section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: The financial impact was severe, with estimated millions in lost productivity and Service Level Agreement penalties across their enterprise customer base.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The outage was severe and disruptive, but the module should not assign unsourced dollar losses or SLA penalties.
claims.rationale: This is fake-precise impact language without a source and should be collapsed to the operational lesson.

claims.claim_text: A major streaming service had a comparable migration incident in late 2022.
claims.span_hint: line 29 | section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: A similar scenario unfolded for a major streaming service in late 2022.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Teams often encounter similar migration blockers when large stateful systems remain after stateless services have moved.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed anecdote about a real company and time period with no verifiable source.

claims.claim_text: That streaming service's remaining footprint included a 12TB PostgreSQL database, 3TB Elasticsearch, 200GB Redis, and 50TB of media files.
claims.span_hint: line 29 | section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: The remaining legacy footprint was a 12TB PostgreSQL database, a 3TB Elasticsearch cluster, a Redis cluster with 200GB of hot data, and a media processing service with 50TB of files on local network storage.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The remaining legacy footprint included large database, search, cache, and file-storage systems that were impractical to move in a single maintenance window.
claims.rationale: These concrete capacities are presented as a real case but are not attributable to any source.

claims.claim_text: Each attempted migration for that service was estimated at two weeks of downtime.
claims.span_hint: line 29 | section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: Every migration attempt was estimated at two weeks of downtime, which the business flatly rejected.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Each proposed cutover exceeded the maintenance window the business was willing to accept.
claims.rationale: The exact downtime estimate is an uncited quantitative anecdote.

claims.claim_text: That database grew from 12TB to 16TB while the migration was delayed.
claims.span_hint: line 31 | section: Why This Module Matters
claims.suggested_rewrite: Meanwhile, the database kept growing, making the eventual migration even harder.
claims.rationale: This is another fake-precise anecdotal number with no attributable source.

claims.claim_text: That team ultimately succeeded with CDC, incremental file synchronization, and the Strangler Fig pattern.
claims.span_hint: line 31 | section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: They eventually succeeded by combining Change Data Capture for the database, incremental synchronization for the media files, and the Strangler Fig pattern to route traffic incrementally.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A workable approach is to combine CDC for the database, incremental synchronization for file stores, and incremental traffic shifting to reduce cutover risk.
claims.rationale: The operational lesson is useful, but it is attached to an unattributed composite war story.

claims.claim_text: The term "data gravity" was coined by Dave McCrory.
claims.span_hint: line 35 | section: Understanding Data Gravity and Escape Velocity
claims.rationale: The primary source is Dave McCrory's original Data Gravitas post, which is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The Strangler Fig pattern is named after the strangler fig tree and describes incremental replacement of a legacy system.
claims.span_hint: line 77 | section: The Strangler Fig Pattern in Practice
claims.rationale: Martin Fowler's original write-up is the right primary source, but the host is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: A Kubernetes ExternalName Service maps a Service to a DNS name and works as a DNS-level alias rather than a proxied backend.
claims.span_hint: line 106 | section: The Strangler Fig Pattern in Practice
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes Service documentation directly describes ExternalName as DNS CNAME-based mapping.

claims.claim_text: Istio supports percentage-based traffic shifting to gradually move requests between service versions.
claims.span_hint: line 161 | section: The Strangler Fig Pattern in Practice
claims.rationale: Istio's traffic shifting task explicitly documents weight-based gradual routing.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes volume snapshots are provided through the VolumeSnapshot API and are available only for CSI drivers.
claims.span_hint: line 224 | section: Infrastructure-Level Migration: CSI Volume Snapshots
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes volume snapshots documentation directly states the CSI-only support model.

claims.claim_text: Amazon EBS snapshots are point-in-time copies stored in S3 and can be used to restore a new EBS volume.
claims.span_hint: line 226 | section: Infrastructure-Level Migration: CSI Volume Snapshots
claims.rationale: AWS documents EBS snapshots as point-in-time copies stored in S3 that can restore new volumes.

claims.claim_text: Amazon EBS snapshots can be copied to another AWS Region.
claims.span_hint: line 255 | section: Infrastructure-Level Migration: CSI Volume Snapshots
claims.rationale: AWS's EBS copy-snapshot documentation directly supports cross-Region copying.

claims.claim_text: Restoring a PVC from a snapshot fully hydrates the block data before the pod starts.
claims.span_hint: line 280 | section: Infrastructure-Level Migration: CSI Volume Snapshots
claims.anchor_text: The CSI driver handles the allocation of the disk and the hydration of the block data prior to the pod starting.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The CSI driver handles provisioning a volume from the snapshot; depending on the backend, blocks may continue initializing after the volume is attached.
claims.rationale: This overstates backend behavior; AWS explicitly documents post-creation volume initialization from snapshots.

claims.claim_text: Logical replication is row-level replication that supports INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, and TRUNCATE, but not schema DDL.
claims.span_hint: line 340 | section: Pattern 2: Logical Replication (Medium Databases)
claims.rationale: AWS Prescriptive Guidance summarizes PostgreSQL logical replication behavior and limits directly.

claims.claim_text: PostgreSQL logical replication can target a different operating system, different major version, or a managed cloud platform.
claims.span_hint: line 340 | section: Pattern 2: Logical Replication (Medium Databases)
claims.rationale: The exact cross-platform wording is best supported by the PostgreSQL manual, which is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Logical replication begins with initial data synchronization and then continues with ongoing replication from the source.
claims.span_hint: line 372 | section: Pattern 2: Logical Replication (Medium Databases)
claims.rationale: AWS DMS documentation for PostgreSQL homogeneous migrations directly describes initial synchronization followed by ongoing replication.

claims.claim_text: CloudNativePG can bootstrap from object-store backups via recovery or from a live source via pg_basebackup.
claims.span_hint: line 464 | section: Pattern 4: Kubernetes Operator Migration
claims.rationale: The CloudNativePG bootstrap documentation directly supports this, but the host is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Logical replication does not replicate sequence state, so cutovers must explicitly reconcile sequences before write traffic resumes.
claims.span_hint: line 525 | section: Zero-Downtime Cutover Runbooks and Verification
claims.rationale: AWS Prescriptive Guidance explicitly lists non-replicated sequences as a logical replication limitation.

claims.claim_text: The most common AWS DMS migration path is Oracle-to-PostgreSQL, followed by SQL Server-to-Aurora.
claims.span_hint: line 576 | section: Did You Know?
claims.anchor_text: The most common migration path is Oracle-to-PostgreSQL, followed by SQL Server-to-Aurora.
claims.suggested_rewrite: AWS DMS supports common heterogeneous migration paths, but the module should not rank source-target pairs without a cited dataset.
claims.rationale: This ranking claim needs dataset-level evidence and none was found on the allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Martin Fowler coined the Strangler Fig pattern in 2004 after seeing strangler fig trees in Australia.
claims.span_hint: line 577 | section: Did You Know?
claims.rationale: Fowler's original 2004 write-up directly supports the date and Australia anecdote, but it is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The Strangler Fig pattern is the default migration strategy for monolith-to-microservices transitions.
claims.span_hint: line 577 | section: Did You Know?
claims.anchor_text: The pattern has become the default migration strategy for monolith-to-microservices transitions.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The pattern is widely used for incremental modernization, but the module should avoid calling it the default strategy without evidence.
claims.rationale: This is an uncited superlative claim about industry practice.

claims.claim_text: At 1 Gbps, 1TB transfers in about 2.5 hours.
claims.span_hint: line 578 | section: Did You Know?
claims.anchor_text: **Data transfer over the internet at 1 Gbps takes about 2.5 hours for 1TB of data.**
claims.suggested_rewrite: At gigabit-class speeds, transferring multi-terabyte datasets over the network still takes many hours even under ideal conditions.
claims.rationale: This exact transfer-time number is presented as an authoritative statistic without a cited primary source.

claims.claim_text: A 50TB database takes over 5 days to transfer at 1 Gbps, and longer in practice.
claims.span_hint: line 578 | section: Did You Know?
claims.anchor_text: For a 50TB database, that's over 5 days of continuous transfer -- assuming the network link is fully saturated, which it won't be.
claims.suggested_rewrite: For datasets in the tens of terabytes, network transfer alone can consume multiple days in ideal conditions and longer in practice.
claims.rationale: This exact duration is uncited arithmetic presented as a fact claim.

claims.claim_text: AWS Snowball Edge transfers 80TB via shipping in 4-6 business days and is competitive above about 50TB.
claims.span_hint: line 578 | section: Did You Know?
claims.anchor_text: AWS Snowball Edge devices transfer 80TB via physical shipping and typically take 4-6 business days door-to-door, making physical transfer competitive or faster for datasets above ~50TB.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Physical transfer appliances can be competitive for very large datasets, but the module should avoid hard-coding device sizes or transit times without a current source.
claims.rationale: The capacity and transit-time details are unstable and not supported as written by a current allowlisted source.

claims.claim_text: Built-in PostgreSQL logical replication was introduced in PostgreSQL 10.
claims.span_hint: line 579 | section: Did You Know?
claims.rationale: AWS's RDS PostgreSQL documentation explicitly states that logical replication was introduced in PostgreSQL 10.

claims.claim_text: Before built-in logical replication, the main near-zero-downtime options were physical replication, Slony, or trigger-based CDC.
claims.span_hint: line 579 | section: Did You Know?
claims.anchor_text: Before logical replication, the primary options for near-zero-downtime migration were: physical replication (requires same PG version and OS), third-party tools like Slony (complex, fragile), or CDC via trigger-based capture (high overhead).
claims.suggested_rewrite: Before built-in logical replication, teams commonly relied on physical replication or third-party tooling, each with additional compatibility or operational tradeoffs.
claims.rationale: The categorical ranking and detailed characterizations need stronger source support than was found on the allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Logical replication made cross-version, cross-platform, and cross-schema PostgreSQL migration routine.
claims.span_hint: line 579 | section: Did You Know?
claims.anchor_text: Logical replication made it possible to replicate between different PG versions, different platforms (on-prem to cloud), and even different schemas -- transforming database migration from a high-risk event to a routine operation.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Built-in logical replication expanded PostgreSQL migration options across versions and platforms, but cutovers still depend on schema compatibility and disciplined validation.
claims.rationale: The concluding rhetoric overstates the evidence even though part of the technical capability is real.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes Volume Snapshots
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers the CSI snapshot model, lifecycle objects, and restoration flow used in the infrastructure migration section.

further_reading.title: AWS Prescriptive Guidance: PostgreSQL Logical Replication
further_reading.why_relevant: Summarizes logical replication behavior and migration limitations, including sequences and schema constraints.

further_reading.title: Amazon EBS Snapshot Copy
further_reading.why_relevant: Directly supports the cross-Region snapshot-copy workflow used in the CSI snapshot migration example.
